And this is the big secret. And again, not her audience, but I know enough about evolutionary psychology. And as long as I can remember, I've been a man. And I know from a male perspective, at least my perspective, men are not the ones who generally feel like they have power in dating relationships. It's generally, and I say all the time, we're not the most sophisticated creatures on earth, we're not. But to me, this is a really, this is a big reframe, right? Because it sounds like women are losing hope. They're attracting the wrong guys. They want to know where, like, I think this is the subject where all the good men, like that's a question, that's a subject line. Is that a thing? That's a subject line. That's a question. Yeah, that's it. That to me frames everything. We're all the good men because you can do an interesting thing when you start to frame things a certain way. You can frame it as where all the good men. And then you can say, the reframe, let's think about this one way. So here's the conventional framing. Where are all the good men because I as the woman need to go out and attract the good men, not deal with all the dumbasses, right? But later you say, here's the thing though, as a woman you choose, right? Men are competing for your attention. You're actually the one in the position of power. So the question isn't where are all the good men? It's how do you put yourself out into the world so that the good men show up for you to choose from? That's an entirely different perspective. Because this to me feels very conventional, right? It's like, okay, you've tried all these things but they've tried all of these things because they have the wrong idea. This to me is a perfect example of this. It's this idea from Rich Sheffrin that Andre knows I just absolutely love, which not to go down too deep into the weeds, but it's a way to think about marketing where, and Rich is the first person I know of who codified this, but the idea is what you think is the problem is X, so you do Y, but the real problem is Z and Y makes Z worse. Everyone who's listening is like, what the hell did he just say? What you think is the problem is that you need to go out and be attractive to the good men. And because of that, you do all these things like you look at these tips and tricks and you read the articles and you go on all the apps and you do all these things that are perfectly logical, but the problem is you framed it the wrong way. You're not out there looking for good men. You are making yourself visible and the good men need to show up and find you. So all the things you were doing because you thought about this the wrong way are the wrong things and they're making the situation worse. And once you see reality for what it is, then you realize they're entirely different things that you should be doing, right? And assuming that this is true, and I believe it's true, certainly from my experience on one side of the equation, it is absolutely true that men are competing for the attention of women. That's what dating feels like, insofar as I can remember what dating ever felt like, but that in general terms, that seems to be kind of how things work. So this is a perception, this is a framing switch. And what they think is the problem is they don't have the power. So they do all of these things because they don't think they have the power, but the reality is they have the power. And once they start making decisions from within that framework of having the power, everything changes. That to me is what this bridge email is about. And sure, yeah, Anna had a baby, she's been gone, that's great, and not to be a jackass, but at the end of the day, I'm gonna show you the audience is connected with Anna and that's important, but this is where I think where I'm a little weird about things. Yeah, I, yeah, I just, here's the thing I'm not gonna read through this entire testimonial here. Just be cautious. I guess this is what I'll wrap up with because I know we've been going for a while, but you need to know what the thing is about either before you write it or after you write it. Like to me, this is a great draft. There's tons of material here. There's no, we can't overstate the value of actually doing the work. So don't take it, any of this is critical. This is just how can we make it better? But you need a thing first to be made better. So just take that, all the commentary and feedback within that lens that you've done the work. This is just commentary about work done. But I would figure out what this is about. Like what's the controlling idea? I would make this three emails not to, and I don't know if this has already been sent, but I would make this, if I were doing this, it would be three emails not to, when you know number one is Anna's back, and here's why this is great. And woo, this is awesome. And now, and now that Anna's back, shit's about to get real. Like that's the first one, right? Things are about to happen. Because Anna's had some time off. She's had plenty of time to be thinking about things. She's had lots of time to be, to realize how great life is for her because of some decisions that she's made. Like excellent, Anna's back. That's email one, email two. Now that Anna's back, we're gonna start talking about some stuff. And here's, and then she's gonna frame it. And what she's realized that since she's been out is that there's a paradigm problem, right? Women think that they're, like they don't realize that they have all the power in a dating relationship. And when you frame it that way, everything that follows makes sense. Once women realize that they have the power in the dating space, that they can, when they change, when they have the power that they can make changes to themselves, that that's what's going to change their reality. That's the frame, that's the controlling idea. Everything needs to fit within that frame. It needs to leave from one to the next to the next. So it's a congruent, cohesive, well-structured argument. Even though you're not framing it as an argument, you're still making a case. And that case is you're thinking about this the wrong way. And because you're thinking about this the wrong way, you're getting all these problems. The moment you think about it the right way, all of those problems go away and all the solutions become available to you. And we're going to talk about what some of those are. And we're going to kind of get you so you understand them. But then to really do it right, we're going to spend 90 minutes together in a master class and we're going to go into detail about this. That's the series. That's what these bridge emails are. Yeah. So. Do you take us home? You know, so there's, according to this Google doc, there's 59 pages of stuff here. And we've spent all of our time on the first two emails, the bridge emails. So, oh, wow, look at that. You know, having a very successful promotion starts with the framing of a really good bridge, you know, bridge series, whether it's one email, one bridge email, the two bridge emails, or it's been set up in a previous email, whether it's a newsletter or something. But we've spent all this time, I don't know how long we've been rambling on now for just for these bridge emails. And you can see that the entire structure is different from our point of view now. And I think the next time they do this promotion, if they model some of what we've been saying here, it'll be interesting to contrast their results to, you know, what happened with this version. I think that'll be interesting insight. But I think, you know, broadly speaking to make this available to everybody, that's, you know, that's not necessarily in the dating space is just to really figure out the strategy behind what you're saying, the controlling idea, the theme, the how this is gonna be structured, and then do it in a way, you know, using these bridge emails to really pull people's attention and get them excited, you know, establish that point of tension, that only the series or the thing behind this, you know, whatever it is, is gonna resolve, you know. So this is the start of establishing that tension. And as soon as we relieve tension inside of the thing that we're trying to get attention from, then that's a red herring, I mean, you know, that's a signal that there's some work that needs to be done. So yeah, I think that's my last words. Yeah, I'm not commenting yet. Yeah, I mean, this was a fun one. Thanks, thanks Kara, for the opportunity to do this. Yeah, this is, it's a big deal. I mean, I know I spent hours yesterday trying to craft a base of a bridge email unsuccessfully. This is hard, it's hard work. And I'll be 100% upfront. I don't want anybody to see that work, right? Like I don't, I would not wanna put that out in the world and get feedback on it. So please hear our feedback for what it is, it's feedback. We don't know your audience. We don't know the context you do. So a lot of what we said may not be accurate, may not fit just right. So but mine it for what's useful and see what happens.
